title: Needle-felted woodland mushroom set
subtitle: Three small mushrooms in brown and ochre merino on a moss-felt base
excerpt: Three needle-felted mushrooms on a small moss-felt base make a compact woodland scene. The technique builds each cap and stalk separately, then joins them with further needling. The project covers basic needle-felted 3D shaping, colour blending, and surface texturing in one session.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted miniature scene work is an established contemporary craft practice. Technique is observation-led.

content.text: Each mushroom consists of a domed cap and a cylindrical stalk, felted separately and joined. Three caps of different sizes (roughly 3 cm, 2.5 cm, and 2 cm in diameter) create variety in a small scene. The base is a flat oval of felted green roving, about 10 cm across, onto which the stalks are needled.

attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Keep a folded sponge under the supporting hand; never needle over fingers or a hard surface.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Merino roving, brown (mid-brown and dark brown)
Felting mat (dense foam, at least 4 cm thick)

content.text: Take the moss-green roving and form a rough oval, approximately 10 cm x 7 cm. Needle all over with the coarse needle until the oval holds its shape and the surface is firm. Set aside.

For each stalk, take a small length of ochre roving (about 3 g). Roll it firmly between your palms into a cylinder. Place on the mat and needle with the medium needle, rolling the cylinder slightly between strokes to felt all sides evenly. Finished stalks should be 2 to 3 cm tall and firm enough not to bend.

For each cap, ball up a piece of brown roving. Needle the ball on the mat, rotating it with fingers as you work, until it forms a compact dome. The underside of the cap should be flat; press it against the mat and needle straight down to flatten it. The top should be gently curved, not perfectly round.
Step 4: Colour blending on the caps
Lay a thin wisp of dark brown roving across the top of each cap. Needle it in with the fine needle. Apply a ring of ochre around the edge of the underside for the gill zone.
at the margins softens the boundary between tones naturally.
Place each stalk on the green base. Needle through the bottom of the stalk and into the base to join them. Place a cap on top of each stalk and needle through the cap into the stalk top. The
pulls fibres from the cap into the stalk surface, forming a firm join without glue.

attrs.intro: Three common problems in needle-felted miniature work.

items.fix: Needle straight down through the cap into the stalk at multiple angles, 12 to 15 strokes per direction, until the join is firm.
items.cause: Join was not fully needled through both pieces.

items.fix: Roll the stalk on the mat and needle from all four sides. The interior fibres need to be reached; use the coarse needle first, then the medium needle to refine the surface.
items.cause: Insufficient needling: the core of the cylinder is still loose.
items.symptom: Stalk is too floppy to stand upright

items.fix: Use a dense foam pad at least 4 cm thick. Stab straight in and out. Replace broken needles immediately: a broken tip inside the felt is a hazard.
items.cause: Needle contacted a hard surface (the table below a thin foam pad) or angled sideways on the stroke.

attrs.heading: Common problems
